title: Emergency Roof Tarp & Dry-In in Shreveport, LA | Commercial Roofers of Shreveport
metaDescription: Emergency tarp and dry-in response for Shreveport-Bossier buildings after storm damage, focused on stopping water intrusion and protecting tenant space fast.

html:
Getting a Building Dry Fast After the Roof Opens Up

When wind tears a membrane loose or a limb comes through the deck, the first job isn't the permanent fix, it's stopping water from getting any further into the building. We show up to secure the opening, protect what's underneath, and buy time to plan the real repair without the ceiling below turning into its own problem.

These calls almost never come with advance notice, and they rarely come in one at a time during an active storm system. We keep crews and material staged specifically so a wind event that opens up multiple roofs across the area doesn't leave any one building waiting longer than it should for a response.

What We're Actually Doing in the First Few Hours

The priority sequence on any emergency call is the same regardless of cause, whether it's a wind-torn membrane, a fallen limb, or a piece of blown rooftop equipment: secure the breach against continued water intrusion, protect tenant space and equipment directly below, and document what we're seeing before we cover it up. That documentation matters later, whether for a repair scope or an insurance record, so we photograph the damage as we go rather than after the fact, before tarps and plywood cover up exactly what needs to be on record.

We're not trying to make the roof look finished on an emergency call. A tarp tied down properly and a section of exposed deck covered with plywood is the right outcome for the first visit, even if it isn't pretty. The goal is dry and stable, not permanent, and we tell you that plainly before we leave the site.

What's underneath the breach changes how we prioritize the response. A retail floor with some water on carpet is a different urgency level than a server room, an operating suite, or a casino floor with electrical equipment at stake. We ask about what's directly below before we're even on site so the crew shows up with the right approach already in mind.

What We Bring on an Emergency Call

Because these calls come in with little notice, our trucks carry what a real dry-in requires without waiting on a supply run:

  • Reinforced tarps and battens sized to the opening
  • Temporary flashing material for torn membrane edges
  • Water extraction equipment for interior standing water
  • Plywood for exposed or damaged deck sections
  • Photo documentation equipment for the insurance record
  • Sandbags and ballast for securing tarps against continued wind
  • Generator-powered tools for after-hours or power-outage calls

Coordinating With Your Building Engineer and Tenants During the Response

We check in with whoever's on site before we start work, not after, both to understand what's happening below the breach and to flag anything that looks like a life-safety concern, exposed wiring, ceiling tile sagging under water weight, that kind of thing. We keep staging out of exits and parking wherever the site allows, since an emergency response shouldn't create a second inconvenience on top of the first.

For buildings with round-the-clock operations, like a hotel, a hospital wing, or a casino floor, we coordinate directly with whoever's running the overnight shift rather than waiting for a business-hours contact. A dry-in crew working over an occupied, operating space at 2 a.m. needs someone on-site who can speak for the building in the moment, and we ask for that contact before we ever mobilize.

What Happens After the Building Is Dry

Once the immediate breach is secured and the building's dry, we walk the site with you or your property manager to talk through what a permanent repair actually looks like and roughly when it needs to happen. If the damage is limited, that might be a scheduled patch within days. If it's extensive, we hand off the documentation we've already gathered so it feeds straight into the repair scope and any insurance conversation, without starting the paperwork over from scratch.

We also use that walk-through to talk honestly about timeline. Some permanent repairs can happen within days once material is on hand; others, especially if the emergency exposed a bigger problem with an aging roof, turn into a longer conversation about full repair or replacement scope. Either way, you leave that walk-through knowing what's next instead of waiting on a callback.

Questions We Get on Emergency Calls

How fast can you get someone on site after hours?

We treat active water intrusion as a same-visit priority regardless of the hour, and response time depends mainly on how far the crew has to travel and current storm conditions across the area.

Is a tarp a permanent fix?

No, it's a temporary measure to stop water intrusion until we can scope and schedule the actual repair. We tell you upfront that it's not the end of the job.

Do you document damage while you're securing the roof?

Yes, we photograph the damage before covering it, since that record matters for both the repair scope and any insurance discussion you need to have afterward.

What if the storm knocked out power on site?

Our crews carry generator-powered tools for exactly that situation, so a power outage doesn't delay the response.

Will you work with our insurance adjuster during the emergency phase?

We can walk the roof with an adjuster if you'd like us there, and we'll answer factual questions about what we found and secured, though the claim itself is handled between you and your carrier.
